title: Chicken bhuna
subtitle: Dry-fried spiced chicken in a thick, clinging masala.
excerpt: Chicken thighs cooked in a masala that is fried down until almost dry and intensely flavoured, then added back to the pan to finish. A bhuna has very little sauce; what there is clings to the meat. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Chicken bhuna. The name derives from the Hindi/Urdu 'bhoona' (to roast or fry), describing the technique of cooking spices and sauce until dry. The dish is associated with North Indian and Anglo-Indian cooking traditions. Madhur Jaffrey's Indian Cookery (1982) describes the bhunoing technique in detail.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to three months. Reheat on the hob with a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. A reliable meal-prep curry.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before; the flavour improves overnight.

content.text: Bhuna describes the technique as much as the dish. The masala is cooked at a higher temperature than most curries, kept moving in the pan, until the sauce has almost completely reduced and the oil starts to separate around the edges. At this point the spices are fully cooked out and the flavour is concentrated. The chicken is added to this thick paste, not to a liquid sauce.

items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, cut into large pieces

content.text: Heat {{rapeseed-oil}} of oil in a large, heavy pan over medium heat. Add {{onion}} chopped onions and cook for 12 minutes until deep golden. Add {{garlic}} of grated garlic and {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger; cook for 3 minutes. Add {{chilli-powder}} of chilli powder, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander and {{turmeric}} of turmeric; stir and fry for 2 minutes.

Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes. Raise the heat to medium-high and cook, stirring frequently, for 15 to 18 minutes. The sauce will bubble vigorously, then thicken, then begin to dry out. Keep cooking until the tomatoes have broken down completely and the oil separates around the edges of the masala. The mixture should look almost dry.

Add {{chicken-thigh}} of chicken pieces and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir to coat in the masala. Add 150 ml of water. Cover and cook over medium-low heat for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce clings thickly. Stir in {{garam-masala}} of garam masala and cook for 2 more minutes uncovered. Scatter coriander to serve.

items.cause: Masala not reduced far enough before adding chicken.
items.fix: The bhunoing stage is critical. Cook until the tomato is completely dry and the oil separates; only then add the chicken.

items.cause: Ground spices not cooked out in the fat.
items.fix: Fry the dry spices in oil for at least 2 minutes before adding the tomatoes. They should smell cooked and aromatic, not raw and powdery.

items.cause: Cooked too long after the bhunoing stage.
items.fix: Chicken thighs take about 20 minutes over medium-low heat with the lid on. Check at 15 minutes.

content.text: Bhuna is a staple of the British curry house, where it usually appears as the 'dry' option on the menu. Made at home, it is a faster curry than a slow-braised rogan josh and a better weeknight option: the bhunoing technique concentrates the flavours quickly without needing long simmering. Serve with basmati rice or a plain paratha.
